title: Minimum path visiting all nodes (graph state DP).
description: Minimum path visiting all nodes (graph state DP).

examples.explanation: Step 1: Compute all-pairs shortest paths using Floyd-Warshall. Initial dist matrix: [[0,1,10],[1,0,2],[10,2,0]]. After Floyd-Warshall: same since all paths are direct. Step 2: Generate all permutations of nodes [0,1,2]: [0,1,2], [0,2,1], [1,0,2], [1,2,0], [2,0,1], [2,1,0]. Step 3: Compute costs: [0,1,2]: dist(0,1)=1 + dist(1,2)=2 = 3. [0,2,1]: dist(0,2)=10 + dist(2,1)=2 = 12. [1,0,2]: dist(1,0)=1 + dist(0,2)=10 = 11. [1,2,0]: dist(1,2)=2 + dist(2,0)=10 = 12. [2,0,1]: dist(2,0)=10 + dist(0,1)=1 = 11. [2,1,0]: dist(2,1)=2 + dist(1,0)=1 = 3. Step 4: Minimum cost = 3.

Step 1: Floyd-Warshall on dist matrix [[0,INF,5],[INF,0,INF],[5,INF,0]]. After: dist[0][1] remains INF, dist[1][0] remains INF, others unchanged. Step 2: Permutations: [0,1,2], [0,2,1], [1,0,2], [1,2,0], [2,0,1], [2,1,0]. Step 3: Compute costs: [0,1,2]: dist(0,1)=INF → cost INF. [0,2,1]: dist(0,2)=5 + dist(2,1)=INF → INF. [1,0,2]: dist(1,0)=INF → INF. [1,2,0]: dist(1,2)=INF → INF. [2,0,1]: dist(2,0)=5 + dist(0,1)=INF → INF. [2,1,0]: dist(2,1)=INF → INF. Step 4: All costs INF, so result INF.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n! * n)
approaches.summary: Generate all permutations of node orders, compute the path cost for each permutation using shortest path distances between nodes (e.g., via Floyd-Warshall), and track the minimum total cost. This is straightforward but impractical for large graphs due to factorial growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Minimum Path Visiting All Nodes
ladders.desc: Complete solution generating all permutations of node orders and computing minimum path cost using shortest path distances.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the Hamiltonian path problem with weighted edges. The intuition is that to visit all nodes exactly once with minimum total distance, we need to consider every possible order of visiting the nodes. For n nodes, there are n! possible permutations. For each permutation, we compute the total path cost by summing the shortest path distances between consecutive nodes in the permutation. We use Floyd-Warshall algorithm to precompute all-pairs shortest path distances, which handles cases where direct edges might not exist between nodes in the permutation. The algorithm works by: 1) Compute all-pairs shortest path distances using Floyd-Warshall. 2) Generate all permutations of nodes (excluding starting node if specified). 3) For each permutation, compute total path cost by summing distances between consecutive nodes. 4) Track the minimum cost found. Time complexity is O(n! * n + n³) where n! * n comes from evaluating permutations and n³ from Floyd-Warshall. Space complexity is O(n²) for the distance matrix. This connects to L1 by using the permutation generation as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle cases where no path exists between nodes (should return infinity), not considering all possible starting nodes if not specified, and inefficient permutation generation. Real code pattern: def min_path_brute_force(graph): n = len(graph); dist = floyd_warshall(graph); min_cost = INF; for perm in permutations(range(n)): cost = 0; for i in range(n-1): if dist[perm[i]][perm[i+1]] == INF: cost = INF; break; cost += dist[perm[i]][perm[i+1]]; min_cost = min(min_cost, cost); return min_cost.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute all-pairs shortest paths using Floyd-Warshall. Initial dist matrix: [[0,1,10],[1,0,2],[10,2,0]]. After Floyd-Warshall: same since all paths are direct. Step 2: Generate all permutations of nodes [0,1,2]: [0,1,2], [0,2,1], [1,0,2], [1,2,0], [2,0,1], [2,1,0]. Step 3: Compute costs: [0,1,2]: dist(0,1)=1 + dist(1,2)=2 = 3. [0,2,1]: dist(0,2)=10 + dist(2,1)=2 = 12. [1,0,2]: dist(1,0)=1 + dist(0,2)=10 = 11. [1,2,0]: dist(1,2)=2 + dist(2,0)=10 = 12. [2,0,1]: dist(2,0)=10 + dist(0,1)=1 = 11. [2,1,0]: dist(2,1)=2 + dist(1,0)=1 = 3. Step 4: Minimum cost = 3.

Step 1: Floyd-Warshall on dist matrix [[0,INF,5],[INF,0,INF],[5,INF,0]]. After: dist[0][1] remains INF, dist[1][0] remains INF, others unchanged. Step 2: Permutations: [0,1,2], [0,2,1], [1,0,2], [1,2,0], [2,0,1], [2,1,0]. Step 3: Compute costs: [0,1,2]: dist(0,1)=INF → cost INF. [0,2,1]: dist(0,2)=5 + dist(2,1)=INF → INF. [1,0,2]: dist(1,0)=INF → INF. [1,2,0]: dist(1,2)=INF → INF. [2,0,1]: dist(2,0)=5 + dist(0,1)=INF → INF. [2,1,0]: dist(2,1)=INF → INF. Step 4: All costs INF, so result INF.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generating All Permutations of Nodes
ladders.desc: Generate all possible orders (permutations) in which nodes can be visited.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine generates all n! permutations of n nodes, which represents all possible paths that visit each node exactly once. The intuition is that for the traveling salesman problem (or Hamiltonian path), the order of visitation matters, and we need to consider every possible sequence. We use backtracking or Heap's algorithm to generate permutations efficiently. Step-by-step: 1) Start with initial list of nodes [0,1,...,n-1]. 2) Use recursion to swap elements and generate all permutations. 3) For each complete permutation, it will be processed by the main algorithm. Time complexity is O(n! * n) to generate and store permutations, but can be O(n!) with constant work per permutation if processed on-the-fly. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack. This connects to L0 as the core enumeration mechanism. Common mistakes: generating permutations with duplicates if nodes have same labels, not handling large n due to factorial growth, and inefficient implementation. Real code pattern: def generate_permutations(arr, start, result): if start == len(arr): result.append(arr.copy()); return; for i in range(start, len(arr)): arr[start], arr[i] = arr[i], arr[start]; generate_permutations(arr, start+1, result); arr[start], arr[i] = arr[i], arr[start].

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with [0,1,2]. Step 2: Recursively generate: Fix 0 at position 0: permute [1,2] → [0,1,2] and [0,2,1]. Fix 1 at position 0: swap 0 and 1 → [1,0,2]; permute [0,2] → [1,0,2] and [1,2,0]; swap back. Fix 2 at position 0: swap 0 and 2 → [2,1,0]; permute [1,0] → [2,1,0] and [2,0,1]; swap back. Step 3: Result has 6 permutations.
Step 1: Start [0,1]. Step 2: Fix 0: permute [1] → [0,1]. Fix 1: swap → [1,0]; permute [0] → [1,0]. Step 3: Result has 2 permutations.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Computing Path Cost for a Given Node Order
ladders.desc: Calculate total distance for a specific permutation using precomputed shortest path distances.
ladders.explanation: This core logic computes the total path cost for a given sequence of nodes by summing the shortest path distances between consecutive nodes in the sequence. The intuition is that once we have a candidate order of visitation, we need to determine the actual travel distance, which may require going through intermediate nodes if direct edges don't exist. We use a precomputed all-pairs shortest path matrix (from Floyd-Warshall) to get distances in O(1) time. Algorithm: 1) Input: permutation list perm and distance matrix dist. 2) Initialize total_cost = 0. 3) For i from 0 to len(perm)-2: get d = dist[perm[i]][perm[i+1]]; if d is INF, break and return INF; else total_cost += d. 4) Return total_cost. Time complexity is O(n) for a permutation of length n. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 as each generated permutation is evaluated using this logic. Common mistakes: not checking for INF distances (which makes path impossible), off-by-one errors in loop bounds, and misunderstanding that dist matrix already accounts for shortest paths. Real code pattern: def path_cost(perm, dist): cost = 0; for i in range(len(perm)-1): if dist[perm[i]][perm[i+1]] == INF: return INF; cost += dist[perm[i]][perm[i+1]]; return cost.

examples.trace: Step 1: cost=0. Step 2: i=0: dist[0][1]=1, cost=1. Step 3: i=1: dist[1][2]=2, cost=3. Step 4: Return 3.
Step 1: cost=0. Step 2: i=0: dist[0][2]=5, cost=5. Step 3: i=1: dist[2][1]=INF, return INF.

ladders.title: Building Block — Floyd-Warshall All-Pairs Shortest Path
ladders.desc: Compute shortest path distances between every pair of nodes in a graph.
ladders.explanation: Floyd-Warshall algorithm computes the shortest path distances between all pairs of nodes in a weighted graph, handling positive weights and detecting unreachable nodes (INF). The intuition is dynamic programming: dist[i][j] is updated by considering if going through an intermediate node k gives a shorter path. Algorithm: 1) Initialize dist matrix from adjacency matrix (0 for i=j, weight for edges, INF otherwise). 2) For k from 0 to n-1: for i from 0 to n-1: for j from 0 to n-1: if dist[i][k] + dist[k][j] < dist[i][j], update dist[i][j] = dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]. 3) Return dist. Time complexity O(n³), space O(n²). This connects to L2 by providing the dist matrix used to compute path costs. Common mistakes: not handling INF correctly in comparisons, incorrect initialization, and using it for graphs with negative cycles. Real code pattern: def floyd_warshall(graph): n = len(graph); dist = [[graph[i][j] for j in range(n)] for i in range(n)]; for k in range(n): for i in range(n): for j in range(n): if dist[i][k] != INF and dist[k][j] != INF: dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]); return dist.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initial dist = [[0,5,INF],[5,0,3],[INF,3,0]]. Step 2: k=0: Update dist[2][1] via 0? No. dist[1][2] via 0? 5+INF=INF no. Step 3: k=1: Update dist[0][2]: 5+3=8 < INF → set to 8. dist[2][0]: 3+5=8 < INF → set to 8. Step 4: k=2: No updates. Final dist as output.
Step 1: Initial dist = input. Step 2: k=0: No updates since INF involved. Step 3: k=1: All INF. Step 4: k=2: No updates. Final unchanged.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Matrix Initialization and Update
ladders.desc: Create and modify a 2D distance matrix for graph algorithms.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves initializing a 2D matrix to represent graph distances and updating its values, which is fundamental to Floyd-Warshall and path cost computation. The intuition is that graphs are often represented as adjacency matrices where matrix[i][j] stores the distance from node i to node j. Initialization: set diagonal to 0 (distance to self), edges to their weights, and non-edges to INF (a large number representing unreachable). Update: in algorithms like Floyd-Warshall, we update matrix[i][j] by comparing with alternative paths. Steps: 1) Define INF as a large constant (e.g., float('inf')). 2) Create n x n matrix. 3) Initialize based on graph input. 4) In loops, update cells using min() operations. Time complexity depends on operations (O(n²) for initialization, O(n³) for Floyd-Warshall updates). Space complexity O(n²). This connects to L3 as Floyd-Warshall relies on this matrix. Common mistakes: using wrong INF value that causes overflow, not copying matrices correctly, and indexing errors. Real code pattern: INF = float('inf'); n = len(graph); dist = [[0]*n for _ in range(n)]; for i in range(n): for j in range(n): if i == j: dist[i][j] = 0; elif graph[i][j] != 0: dist[i][j] = graph[i][j]; else: dist[i][j] = INF.

examples.trace: Step 1: Set INF = large value. Step 2: Create 3x3 matrix. Step 3: Initialize: i=0,j=0: 0; j=1: graph[0][1]=2; j=2: INF. i=1,j=0: 2; j=1: 0; j=2: 3. i=2,j=0: INF; j=1: 3; j=2: 0.

examples.input: Update dist[0][2] = min(dist[0][2], dist[0][1]+dist[1][2]) with dist=[[0,2,INF],[2,0,3],[INF,3,0]]
examples.trace: Step 1: Current dist[0][2] = INF. Step 2: Compute dist[0][1] + dist[1][2] = 2 + 3 = 5. Step 3: min(INF, 5) = 5. Step 4: Update dist[0][2] to 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation as Adjacency Matrix
ladders.desc: Represent a graph using a 2D matrix where entry [i][j] stores the distance from node i to node j.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves representing a graph as an adjacency matrix, which is a 2D array where rows and columns correspond to nodes, and the value at matrix[i][j] represents the weight of the edge from node i to node j. For unweighted graphs, use 1 for edges and 0 for no edge. For weighted graphs, use the edge weight. If there's no edge, use a special value INF (infinity). The diagonal matrix[i][i] is typically 0, meaning distance from a node to itself is zero. Intuition: this representation allows quick lookup of distances between any two nodes in O(1) time, making it useful for algorithms that need all-pairs information. Step-by-step: 1) Identify nodes numbered 0 to n-1. 2) Create n x n matrix. 3) For each edge from i to j with weight w, set matrix[i][j] = w. 4) Set matrix[i][i] = 0 for all i. 5) Set all other entries to INF. Time to create is O(n²), space is O(n²). This connects to L4 as the initial input for matrix operations. Common mistakes: confusing directed vs undirected graphs (matrix may be symmetric for undirected), using wrong dimensions, and not handling INF properly. Real code pattern: def create_adj_matrix(n, edges): INF = float('inf'); matrix = [[INF]*n for _ in range(n)]; for i in range(n): matrix[i][i] = 0; for (u,v,w) in edges: matrix[u][v] = w; return matrix.

examples.trace: Step 1: Create 3x3 matrix with INF. Step 2: Set diagonals: [0][0]=0, [1][1]=0, [2][2]=0. Step 3: Add edge (0,1,2): set [0][1]=2 and [1][0]=2 (undirected). Step 4: Add edge (1,2,3): set [1][2]=3 and [2][1]=3. Step 5: Other entries remain INF.
Step 1: Create 2x2 matrix with INF. Step 2: Set diagonals: [0][0]=0, [1][1]=0. Step 3: Add directed edge (0,1,5): set [0][1]=5. Step 4: [1][0] remains INF.

approaches.name: DP with Bitmask and Floyd-Warshall O(2^n * n^2)
approaches.summary: Use Floyd-Warshall to precompute all-pairs shortest paths, then apply dynamic programming with a bitmask to represent visited nodes. The DP state dp[mask][i] stores the minimum cost to visit nodes in mask ending at node i, transitioning by adding unvisited nodes.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Path Visiting All Nodes with DP and Bitmask
ladders.desc: Complete solution using Floyd-Warshall and DP with bitmask to find shortest path visiting all nodes.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the shortest path that visits every node in a weighted undirected graph at least once, starting and ending anywhere. The challenge is the exponential number of possible visitation orders. The solution combines two key techniques: Floyd-Warshall for all-pairs shortest paths and DP with bitmask for state representation. First, compute shortest distances between all node pairs using Floyd-Warshall (O(n³)). Then define DP state dp[mask][i] = minimum distance to visit all nodes in mask ending at node i. Initialize dp[1<<i][i] = 0 for all starting nodes i. Transition by adding unvisited node j: dp[mask|(1<<j)][j] = min(dp[mask|(1<<j)][j], dp[mask][i] + dist[i][j]). The answer is min(dp[(1<<n)-1][i]) over all i. Time: O(2^n * n²) from DP transitions plus O(n³) for Floyd-Warshall. Space: O(2^n * n) for DP table. The intuition: Floyd-Warshall ensures we always use optimal subpaths between nodes, while DP with bitmask efficiently explores all visitation permutations without redundancy.

examples.trace: Step 1: Floyd-Warshall distances: dist[0][1]=10, dist[0][2]=5, dist[1][2]=20 (already shortest). Step 2: Initialize DP: dp[001][0]=0, dp[010][1]=0, dp[100][2]=0. Step 3: Transitions from mask 001: Add node 1: dp[011][1]=min(inf, 0+10)=10. Add node 2: dp[101][2]=min(inf, 0+5)=5. From mask 010: Add node 0: dp[011][0]=min(inf, 0+10)=10. Add node 2: dp[110][2]=min(inf, 0+20)=20. From mask 100: Add node 0: dp[101][0]=min(inf, 0+5)=5. Add node 1: dp[110][1]=min(inf, 0+20)=20. Step 4: From mask 011: Add node 2: dp[111][2]=min(inf, dp[011][0]+5=15, dp[011][1]+20=30)=15. From mask 101: Add node 1: dp[111][1]=min(inf, dp[101][0]+10=15, dp[101][2]+20=25)=15. From mask 110: Add node 0: dp[111][0]=min(inf, dp[110][1]+10=30, dp[110][2]+5=25)=25. Step 5: Answer = min(dp[111][0]=25, dp[111][1]=15, dp[111][2]=15)=15. Wait, recalc: Actually from mask 011: dp[011][0]=10, dp[011][1]=10. Add node 2: dp[111][2]=min(inf, 10+5=15, 10+20=30)=15. From mask 101: dp[101][0]=5, dp[101][2]=5. Add node 1: dp[111][1]=min(inf, 5+10=15, 5+20=25)=15. From mask 110: dp[110][1]=20, dp[110][2]=20. Add node 0: dp[111][0]=min(inf, 20+10=30, 20+5=25)=25. Answer min=15. But path: Start at 0 (0), go to 2 (5), go to 1 (5+20=25) total 25? Check: Actually shortest path: Start at 2, go to 0 (5), go to 1 (5+10=15) total 15. Yes correct.

Step 1: Floyd-Warshall: dist[0][1]=1, dist[0][2]=2, dist[0][3]=2 (via 1), dist[1][2]=3 (via 0), dist[1][3]=1, dist[2][3]=2. Step 2: Initialize DP for 4 nodes (masks 0001 to 1000). Step 3: Build up DP. Key path: Start at 0: dp[0001][0]=0. Add node 1: dp[0011][1]=1. Add node 3: dp[1011][3]=2. Add node 2: dp[1111][2]=4 (via dist[3][2]=2). Alternative: Start at 0, add 2: dp[0101][2]=2, add 3: dp[1101][3]=4, add 1: dp[1111][1]=5. Minimum is 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Transition with Bitmask
ladders.desc: Implement the DP transition logic to build up visited states.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine forms the core of the DP solution. Given precomputed all-pairs shortest distances dist[i][j] and DP table dp[mask][i], we systematically build up from smaller masks to larger ones. The algorithm: 1. Initialize dp[1<<i][i] = 0 for all i (starting at any node costs 0). 2. Iterate over all masks from 1 to (1<<n)-1. 3. For each mask, for each ending node i where dp[mask][i] is not infinity, try adding each unvisited node j (where mask & (1<<j) == 0). 4. Transition: new_mask = mask | (1<<j); dp[new_mask][j] = min(dp[new_mask][j], dp[mask][i] + dist[i][j]). 5. After processing all masks, answer is min(dp[(1<<n)-1][i]) over all i. Time: O(2^n * n²) for nested loops over masks, ending nodes, and next nodes. Space: O(2^n * n) for DP table. The intuition: Each transition represents extending a path that has visited nodes in mask and ends at i by moving to an unvisited node j, using the shortest distance between i and j. This ensures we always consider optimal subpaths. Common mistakes: Not initializing all dp values to infinity, incorrect bitmask operations, or missing the final min over all ending nodes.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bitmask Representation of Visited Nodes
ladders.desc: Using bitmask to efficiently represent subsets of visited nodes.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is representing which nodes have been visited using a bitmask—an integer where the i-th bit is 1 if node i is visited, 0 otherwise. For n nodes, masks range from 0 to (1<<n)-1. This allows O(1) checks for visitation status and compact state representation. Key operations: 1. Set bit i: mask | (1<<i). 2. Check if bit i is set: mask & (1<<i) != 0. 3. Count bits: bin(mask).count('1') or built-in functions. In DP, dp[mask][i] stores minimum cost to visit exactly the nodes in mask ending at i. Transition adds an unvisited node j by setting bit j. The intuition: Instead of storing explicit sets (costly), bitmask allows integer indexing and fast operations. Time: O(1) per bit operation. Space: O(2^n) for masks. Connection: This enables the DP state definition in the full solution. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in bit positions, confusion between 0-indexing and bits, or not handling large n where 2^n may be large.

examples.trace: Node indices: 0,1,2,3. Binary positions: bit0 for node0, bit1 for node1, bit2 for node2, bit3 for node3. Visited nodes: 0 and 2. Set bit0: 1<<0=1, mask=1. Set bit2: 1<<2=4, mask=1|4=5. Binary: 0101 (bits from right: bit0=1, bit1=0, bit2=1, bit3=0). Check node1 visited? mask & (1<<1)=5 & 2=0, false. Add node3: mask | (1<<3)=5 | 8=13, binary 1101.
Binary of 6: 110. Bits from right: bit0=0 (node0 not visited), bit1=1 (node1 visited), bit2=1 (node2 visited). So visited set {1,2}. Check: 1<<1=2, 1<<2=4, sum=6.

ladders.title: Building Block — Floyd-Warshall Algorithm
ladders.desc: Compute all-pairs shortest paths in a weighted graph.
ladders.explanation: Floyd-Warshall finds shortest distances between every pair of nodes in a weighted graph, allowing positive or negative weights (no negative cycles). Input: n nodes, adjacency matrix dist where dist[i][j] = weight of edge i-j or infinity if no edge. Algorithm: Initialize dist[i][j] = 0 if i==j, else weight or inf. Then triple loop: for k in 0..n-1: for i in 0..n-1: for j in 0..n-1: dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]). Intuition: Gradually allow paths through intermediate node k. After iteration k, dist[i][j] is shortest path using nodes 0..k as intermediates. Time: O(n³). Space: O(n²). In the full problem, this precomputation ensures DP transitions use optimal distances between any two nodes, crucial since the path may revisit nodes. Common mistakes: Not initializing diagonal to 0, incorrect infinity values, or wrong loop order.

examples.trace: Initial dist: [[0,4,11],[4,0,2],[11,2,0]]. k=0: No change since no path through 0 improves. k=1: i=0,j=2: dist[0][2]=min(11, dist[0][1]+dist[1][2]=4+2=6) -> 6. Others unchanged. k=2: i=0,j=1: min(4, dist[0][2]+dist[2][1]=6+2=8) no change. Final: [[0,4,6],[4,0,2],[6,2,0]].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over All Subsets with Bitmask
ladders.desc: Systematically enumerate all subsets of n elements using bitmask iteration.
ladders.explanation: A fundamental operation is generating all subsets of a set of size n using bitmasks. Since each subset corresponds to a binary number from 0 to 2^n-1, we can iterate mask from 0 to (1<<n)-1. Each mask represents a subset where bit i is 1 if element i is in the subset. To iterate efficiently: for mask in range(1<<n): process mask. To iterate over masks with specific properties (e.g., non-empty): start from 1. To iterate in increasing size order: for size in range(n+1): for mask with popcount(mask)==size. Popcount can be computed with bin(mask).count('1') or builtins. Intuition: This enumeration allows DP to process states in order of increasing subset size, ensuring dependencies are met. Time: O(2^n) to iterate all masks. Space: O(1). Connection: In the DP solution, we iterate masks from small to large to build up visited sets. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in range, confusion between mask values and indices, or not handling large n where 2^n is huge.

examples.trace: All masks: 0,1,2,3. Popcount: 0->0, 1->1, 2->1, 3->2. Filter popcount==1: masks 1 (01) and 2 (10).

testCases.input: n=3, masks in increasing popcount order

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Representation and Bit Operations
ladders.desc: Understanding binary numbers and basic bit manipulation operations.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers binary representation of integers and essential bit operations. In computing, integers are stored in binary (base-2). For an integer, each bit position represents a power of 2. Key operations: 1. Left shift (<<): x << i shifts bits left by i positions, equivalent to multiplying by 2^i. Example: 1 << 3 = 8. 2. Bitwise OR (|): combines bits; 1 | 0 = 1, 0 | 0 = 0. Used to set bits. 3. Bitwise AND (&): extracts bits; 1 & 1 = 1, otherwise 0. Used to check bits. 4. Bitwise NOT (~): inverts bits. Intuition: These operations allow compact representation and fast manipulation of sets. For example, to represent a set of n elements, use an n-bit integer where bit i=1 means element i is in the set. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1). Connection: This enables bitmask representation in higher levels. Common mistakes: Confusing bit positions (0-indexed from right), overflow with shifts, or misunderstanding operator precedence.

examples.input: Set bits for elements {0,2} in n=4
examples.trace: Element 0: bit position 0, set with 1<<0=1. Element 2: bit position 2, set with 1<<2=4. Combine with OR: 1 | 4 = 5. Binary: 0101 (bits from right: pos0=1, pos1=0, pos2=1, pos3=0). Check element 1: mask & (1<<1)=5 & 2=0, so not in set.
Binary of 13: 1101. Bits from right: pos0=1 (element0 in set), pos1=0 (element1 not), pos2=1 (element2 in), pos3=1 (element3 in). So set {0,2,3}. Verify: 1<<0=1, 1<<2=4, 1<<3=8, sum=13.

approaches.name: DP with Bitmask and BFS/Dijkstra O(2^n * (n + E))
approaches.summary: Similar to the bitmask DP approach but computes shortest paths on-the-fly using BFS (for unweighted graphs) or Dijkstra (for weighted graphs) during DP transitions, avoiding the O(n^3) Floyd-Warshall precomputation. This can be more efficient for sparse graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Path Visiting All Nodes with DP + Bitmask + BFS
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP with bitmask state and BFS/Dijkstra transitions to find shortest path visiting all nodes.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the shortest path that visits every node in a graph at least once, which is essentially the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) for general graphs. The key insight is to use dynamic programming with bitmask state representation combined with BFS (for unweighted graphs) or Dijkstra (for weighted graphs) to compute transitions efficiently. We define dp[mask][node] as the minimum distance to visit all nodes in the mask ending at node. The mask is a bitmask where bit i is 1 if node i has been visited. We initialize dp[1 << i][i] = 0 for all starting nodes i. Then we iterate through all masks from smallest to largest, and for each (mask, node) pair with finite distance, we perform BFS/Dijkstra from node to find distances to other nodes, updating dp[mask | (1 << next)][next] = min(dp[mask][node] + dist(node, next)). This avoids precomputing all-pairs shortest paths with Floyd-Warshall (O(n³)), making it O(2ⁿ * (n + E)) which is better for sparse graphs. The answer is min(dp[(1 << n) - 1][i]) over all i. Intuition: The bitmask tracks visited nodes, and BFS/Dijkstra computes shortest paths on-the-fly during DP transitions, ensuring we always use optimal subpaths. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize dp array with INF, dp[1 << i][i] = 0. 2. For mask from 1 to (1 << n) - 1: For each node where dp[mask][node] is finite: Run BFS/Dijkstra from node to get distances to all nodes. For each next node: new_mask = mask | (1 << next). Update dp[new_mask][next] = min(dp[new_mask][next], dp[mask][node] + dist(node, next)). 3. Answer = min(dp[(1 << n) - 1][i]). Time: O(2ⁿ * (n + E)) from DP states (2ⁿ * n) times BFS/Dijkstra O(n + E). Space: O(2ⁿ * n) for dp array. Connection to L1: This uses the BFS/Dijkstra transition subroutine from L1 to compute distances during DP updates. Common mistakes: Not handling weighted graphs correctly (use Dijkstra instead of BFS), incorrect bitmask operations, or missing INF initialization. Real code pattern: Use 2D array dp[mask][node], BFS queue or priority queue for Dijkstra, bit operations for mask updates.

examples.trace: Graph: 0 connected to 1,2,3 (unweighted). Initialize dp: dp[0001][0]=0, dp[0010][1]=0, dp[0100][2]=0, dp[1000][3]=0. Process mask=0001 (node 0): BFS from 0 gives dist to 1=1,2=1,3=1. Update: mask 0011 (nodes 0,1) at node 1: dp=1; mask 0101 at node 2: dp=1; mask 1001 at node 3: dp=1. Similarly process other masks. Eventually, for mask=1111 (all nodes): dp[1111][0]=4 (path 0->1->0->2->0->3 or similar), dp[1111][1]=4, etc. Min=4.

Complete graph with 3 nodes. Initialize dp: dp[001][0]=0, dp[010][1]=0, dp[100][2]=0. Process mask=001 (node 0): BFS from 0 gives dist to 1=1,2=1. Update: mask 011 at node 1: dp=1; mask 101 at node 2: dp=1. Next, process mask=011 (nodes 0,1) at node 1: BFS from 1 gives dist to 2=1 (via edge 1-2). Update mask 111 at node 2: dp=2. Similarly other paths. Answer min dp[111][i]=2 (e.g., 0->1->2).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS/Dijkstra Transition for DP Updates
ladders.desc: Helper function to compute shortest paths from a node during DP state transitions.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the efficient DP approach: instead of precomputing all-pairs shortest paths, we compute distances on-the-fly when processing a DP state (mask, node). Given a starting node and the current mask (visited nodes), we run BFS (for unweighted graphs) or Dijkstra (for weighted graphs) to find the shortest distance from the starting node to every other node in the graph. These distances are then used to update DP states for extended masks. Intuition: When we are at a certain state (having visited nodes in mask and currently at node), we need to know the cost to move to another node to expand our visited set. By computing shortest paths dynamically, we avoid O(n³) precomputation and leverage graph sparsity. Step-by-step: 1. Input: graph (adjacency list), start node, mask (optional, but can be used to prune if needed). 2. Initialize distance array dist[] with INF, dist[start] = 0. 3. For BFS (unweighted): Use queue, for each node popped, explore neighbors, update dist if not visited. For Dijkstra (weighted): Use priority queue (min-heap), push (0, start), pop smallest distance, relax edges. 4. Output dist array with shortest distances from start to all nodes. Time: O(n + E) for BFS, O((n + E) log n) for Dijkstra. Space: O(n) for dist array and queue/heap. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each DP state (mask, node) to compute transitions, forming the inner loop of the DP algorithm. Common mistakes: Using BFS for weighted graphs (incorrect), not resetting dist array for each call, or inefficient graph representation. Real code pattern: BFS with queue and visited set, or Dijkstra with heapq in Python.

examples.trace: BFS from node 0: Queue: [0], dist[0]=0. Pop 0, neighbors 1 and 2: dist[1]=1, dist[2]=1, queue=[1,2]. Pop 1, neighbors 0 and 3: dist[3]=2 (via 1), queue=[2,3]. Pop 2, neighbors 0 and 3: dist[3] already 2, no update. Pop 3, neighbors 1 and 2: no updates. Final dist=[0,1,1,2].

Dijkstra from node 0: Min-heap: [(0,0)]. Pop (0,0), relax edges: to 1 with weight 2 (dist[1]=2), to 2 with weight 5 (dist[2]=5), heap=[(2,1),(5,2)]. Pop (2,1), relax edges from 1: to 0 (no update), to 2 with weight 1 (dist[2]=min(5,2+1=3)=3), heap=[(3,2),(5,2)]. Pop (3,2), relax edges: to 0 and 1, no updates. Final dist=[0,2,3].

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP State with Bitmask for Visited Nodes
ladders.desc: Using dynamic programming with bitmask to represent subsets of visited nodes.
ladders.explanation: The central technique is to define a DP state dp[mask][node] where mask is a bitmask integer representing which nodes have been visited so far, and node is the current node we are at. This state captures the minimum distance to achieve that configuration. The bitmask has n bits, where bit i is 1 if node i has been visited, and 0 otherwise. This allows us to represent 2ⁿ possible subsets of nodes efficiently. The DP transition is: from state (mask, node), we can move to another node next, updating the mask to include next, and the cost is the distance from node to next. Formally, dp[mask | (1 << next)][next] = min(dp[mask | (1 << next)][next], dp[mask][node] + dist(node, next)). Intuition: This is a state-space search where each state is a (visited set, current position) pair, and we want the shortest path to visit all nodes. The bitmask enables compact representation and efficient iteration over subsets. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize dp with INF, set dp[1 << i][i] = 0 for all i (starting at node i with only i visited). 2. Iterate masks from 1 to (1 << n) - 1 in increasing order (to ensure smaller masks processed first). 3. For each mask and each node where dp[mask][node] is finite, try transitioning to all other nodes next. 4. Update dp for new_mask = mask | (1 << next). 5. After processing all masks, answer is min over dp[(1 << n) - 1][i] (all nodes visited, ending at i). Time: O(2ⁿ * n²) if using precomputed distances, but with BFS/Dijkstra transitions it becomes O(2ⁿ * (n + E)). Space: O(2ⁿ * n). Connection to L1: This DP framework uses the distance computations from L1 for transitions. Common mistakes: Incorrect bitmask operations (e.g., using mask & (1 << i) instead of mask | (1 << i)), not initializing starting states, or iterating masks in wrong order. Real code pattern: 2D array dp[1 << n][n], loops over mask and node, bit operations for mask updates.

examples.trace: For n=3, bitmask length 3. Initialize: dp for mask with single bit set: mask 001 (binary) for node 0: dp[1][0]=0. mask 010 for node 1: dp[2][1]=0. mask 100 for node 2: dp[4][2]=0. All other dp entries set to INF.

examples.input: Transition from mask=001 (node 0) to node 1 with distance 2
examples.trace: Current state: mask=001 (visited node 0), at node 0, dp=0. Moving to node 1: new_mask = 001 | (1 << 1) = 001 | 010 = 011 (binary 3). Distance from node 0 to 1 is 2. So update dp[3][1] = min(current INF, 0 + 2) = 2.

testCases.expected: new_mask = 101, dp[5][0] = 4 (no change in mask since node 0 already visited)

ladders.title: Building Block — Bitmask Operations for Subset Representation
ladders.desc: Using bitwise operations to represent and manipulate subsets of nodes.
ladders.explanation: Bitmask is a technique where an integer's binary representation is used to represent a subset of elements. For n nodes, we use an n-bit integer where the i-th bit (from right, 0-indexed) is 1 if node i is in the subset, and 0 otherwise. This allows efficient storage and manipulation of subsets using bitwise operations. Key operations: 1. Set bit i: mask | (1 << i). 2. Check if bit i is set: (mask >> i) & 1 or mask & (1 << i) != 0. 3. Toggle bit i: mask ^ (1 << i). 4. Iterate over all masks from 0 to (1 << n) - 1. Intuition: This compresses subset information into a single integer, enabling fast DP state representation and transitions. For example, with n=3, mask 5 (binary 101) represents nodes 0 and 2 visited. Step-by-step: To use in DP: Initialize mask for single node: 1 << i. To add a node j: new_mask = mask | (1 << j). To check if all nodes visited: mask == (1 << n) - 1 (all bits set). Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1) per mask. Connection to L2: This bitmask representation is used to define the DP state mask in L2. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in bit indexing (0-index vs 1-index), incorrect bit shifts (e.g., 1 << i for i>=64 on some systems), or misunderstanding binary representation. Real code pattern: Use integers, bitwise OR, AND, left shift.

examples.trace: Nodes: 0,1,2,3. Bits: bit0 for node 0, bit1 for node 1, bit2 for node 2, bit3 for node 3. Subset {0,2}: set bit0 and bit2. mask = (1 << 0) | (1 << 2) = 1 | 4 = 5. Binary: 0101.

examples.input: mask = 6 (binary 0110), check if node 1 is visited
examples.trace: mask = 6, binary 0110. Bits: bit0=0, bit1=1, bit2=1, bit3=0. Check node 1 (bit1): (mask >> 1) & 1 = (6 >> 1) = 3, binary 0011, & 1 = 1. So node 1 is visited.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Graph Representation and Traversal (BFS/Dijkstra)
ladders.desc: Fundamental graph operations: adjacency list representation and shortest path algorithms.
ladders.explanation: Graphs are represented as adjacency lists, where for each node, we store a list of its neighbors. For unweighted graphs, it's a list of node indices; for weighted graphs, it's a list of (neighbor, weight) pairs. This representation is efficient for sparse graphs. Traversal algorithms: BFS (Breadth-First Search) finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs by exploring nodes level by level using a queue. Dijkstra's algorithm finds shortest paths in weighted graphs with non-negative weights using a priority queue (min-heap) to always expand the closest node. Intuition: BFS works because in unweighted graphs, the first time a node is visited is via the shortest path. Dijkstra works by greedily selecting the node with smallest known distance and relaxing its edges. Step-by-step BFS: 1. Initialize queue with start node, dist[start]=0. 2. While queue not empty: pop node, for each neighbor: if not visited, set dist[neighbor] = dist[node] + 1, push to queue. Dijkstra: 1. Initialize min-heap with (0, start), dist[start]=0. 2. While heap not empty: pop (d, node), if d > dist[node], skip; for each (neighbor, weight): if dist[node] + weight < dist[neighbor], update dist[neighbor] and push to heap. Time: BFS O(V + E), Dijkstra O((V + E) log V). Space: O(V) for dist and queue/heap. Connection to L3: These traversals are used to compute distances for bitmask DP transitions. Common mistakes: Forgetting to mark visited in BFS (causing cycles), using BFS for weighted graphs, or incorrect heap operations in Dijkstra. Real code pattern: BFS with collections.deque, Dijkstra with heapq in Python.

examples.trace: Start: queue=[0], dist[0]=0. Pop 0, neighbors 1,2: dist[1]=1, dist[2]=1, queue=[1,2]. Pop 1, neighbors 0,3: dist[3]=2, queue=[2,3]. Pop 2, neighbors 0,3: dist[3] already 2. Pop 3, neighbors 1,2: no update. Distances: [0,1,1,2].

Start: heap=[(0,0)], dist[0]=0. Pop (0,0), relax: to 1 with 2 (dist[1]=2), to 2 with 5 (dist[2]=5), heap=[(2,1),(5,2)]. Pop (2,1), relax from 1: to 2 with 1 (dist[2]=min(5,2+1=3)=3), heap=[(3,2),(5,2)]. Pop (3,2), relax: no updates. Distances: [0,2,3].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graphs and Dynamic Programming Basics
ladders.desc: Introductory concepts: graph terminology and DP fundamentals.
ladders.explanation: A graph is a collection of nodes (vertices) and edges connecting them. Key terms: node, edge, adjacency (neighbors), path (sequence of nodes), distance (number of edges or sum of weights). Graphs can be directed or undirected, weighted or unweighted. Dynamic Programming (DP) is a method to solve problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing results to avoid recomputation. It involves defining a state, base cases, and transition relations. Intuition: Instead of solving from scratch, remember past solutions to build up to the final answer. For example, in Fibonacci, fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2), with base cases fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1. Step-by-step DP: 1. Identify subproblems. 2. Define state representation. 3. Formulate recurrence relation. 4. Determine base cases. 5. Compute values (iteratively or recursively with memoization). Time/space depends on number of states and transitions. Connection to L4: Graph traversal provides distances used in DP transitions for path problems. Common mistakes: Not identifying overlapping subproblems, incorrect state definition, or missing base cases. Real code pattern: Use arrays or dictionaries to store state values, loops to fill DP table.

examples.trace: DP states: dp[0]=0, dp[1]=1. Recurrence: dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]. Compute: dp[2]=1+0=1, dp[3]=1+1=2, dp[4]=2+1=3, dp[5]=3+2=5.

examples.input: Graph with 3 nodes in a line: 0-1-2, find shortest path from 0 to 2
examples.trace: Nodes: 0,1,2. Edges: 0-1, 1-2. Shortest path from 0 to 2: 0->1->2, distance 2 edges.

testCases.input: Graph: nodes 1, no edges, distance 0 to 0
